original_abstract: Selective alkene epoxidation remains a critical challenge in sustainable chemical manufacturing due to the high energy requirements and low efficiency of conventional methods. In this study, we report a highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) system for the epoxidation of diverse alkenes using an $\alpha$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ (hematite) photoanode and a Br$^-$/BrO$^-$ redox mediator. By utilizing water as the sole oxygen source, this strategy achieves exceptional epoxide selectivity (up to >99%) and Faradaic efficiencies (up to 82 $\pm$ 4%), significantly outperforming existing direct electrochemical and PEC oxidation benchmarks. Mechanistic investigations—including isotope labeling, Hammett linear free-energy relationship analysis, and comparative studies with TiO$_2$ and Pt electrodes—reveal a unique non-radical pathway. On the $\alpha$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ surface, bromide is oxidized via oxygen atom transfer (OAT) from high-valent iron-oxo species to form hypobromite (BrO$^-$), which subsequently transfers the oxygen atom to the alkene substrate in the bulk solution. This mediated approach bypasses the radical-induced side reactions and thermodynamic limitations typical of direct oxidation. Our findings demonstrate a robust and energy-efficient PEC platform for the simultaneous production of value-added epoxides and hydrogen, offering a promising route for integrated solar-to-chemical energy conversion.
humanized_abstract: The selective alkene epoxidation remains a highly energy demanding and selective process in catalytic production. Here, we introduce a photoelectrochemical (PEC) process capable of producing various types of epoxides in very high selectivities and faradaic efficiencies. Employing an $\alpha$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ photoanode and a Br$^-$/BrO$^-$ solution redox couple allowed us to achieve highly selective and selective water-based PEC oxidation of alkenes (selectivities, $>$99\% and Faradaic efficiencies up to 82 $\pm$ 4\%). These values surpass those of the direct electrochemical and direct PEC oxidation of alkenes (electro- and photo-oxidation). A detailed analysis of the reaction pathway via isotopic experiments, Hammett analysis and investigations of the photocatalytic activity of TiO$_2$ as well as the performance of a platinum electrode revealed that the oxidation of bromide ions into hypobromite ions occurs at the hematite surface via an oxygen atom transfer (OAT) reaction from iron-oxo species of high valency. These ions lead to the selective oxygen-atom transfer reaction in the solution in which the formed hypobromite ion catalytically brings about the alkene oxidation. The reaction mechanism does not involve any radical species or the high overpotentials that are known to occur in the direct oxidation reactions. Therefore, the novel PEC process based on the hematite photoanode bears great promise to become a competitive technology in catalytic production for making selectively and catalytically valuable epoxides using solar energy. The produced hydrogen can be easily harvested in the H$_2$ chamber of the solar photoanodic fuel cell.
